location.origin: Seattle, Washington -- native-born Seattle musician; the first artist whose entire educational path through the terminal degree is completed within the Seattle metropolitan area
location.formative: University of Washington (DMA in Musical Arts); Seattle jazz performance community; collaborative network with Priester, and other Seattle musicians
location.career: Seattle (entire career) -- University of Washington School of Music faculty, director of jazz studies; Naim Records recording artist; Seattle jazz clubs and festivals; the institutional anchor of the UW jazz program

theory_nodes.concept: Modal jazz piano vocabulary (Dorian, Mixolydian, Lydian scales as compositional and improvisational basis; quartal voicings; extended chord structures; Lydian Dominant color)
examples: Marc Seales -- Naim Records recordings; UW jazz ensemble direction; modal voicings deployed in post-bop contemporary context
McCoy Tyner -- 'Passion Dance': quartal voicings as primary harmonic vocabulary; the source of the contemporary jazz piano sound
Herbie Hancock -- 'Maiden Voyage': modal composition with open voicings; horizontal (scale-based) rather than vertical (chord-based) improvisation
Keith Jarrett -- Koln Concert: extended modal improvisation; the piano as a self-sufficient modal instrument
Chick Corea -- 'Spain': Lydian brightness in melodic construction; the raised fourth as signature contemporary jazz color
theory_nodes.description: Modal jazz piano vocabulary is the post-bebop language that organizes improvisation around scales (modes) rather than chord changes. Core techniques: (1) Modal scales as compositional basis -- Dorian, Mixolydian, Lydian, Aeolian modes sustained for extended periods, allowing deep exploration of modal color rather than rapid chord-change navigation. (2) Quartal voicings -- chords built from stacked fourths (C-F-Bb-Eb) rather than stacked thirds, producing open, ambiguous, modern sound that avoids functional resolution. McCoy Tyner's innovation, now standard vocabulary. (3) Extended chord structures -- 9ths, 11ths, 13ths as standard voicing components, creating rich harmonic textures. (4) Lydian and Lydian Dominant color -- the raised fourth degree (#11) as the signature pitch of contemporary jazz harmony; George Russell's Lydian Chromatic Concept as theoretical foundation. (5) Academic jazz pedagogy as integration point -- the DMA-level understanding that connects performing knowledge with scholarly framework. Seales teaches these concepts within the full institutional apparatus of a research university, transmitting both the physical vocabulary and the theoretical system that generates it.

ancestors: Bebop vocabulary -- chromatic approach, enclosures, tritone substitution (degree 12, Lanphere)
Hard bop piano voicings -- orchestral density (degree 6, Bown)
Stride piano -- left-hand bass patterns (degree 5, Berry)
Miles Davis modal revolution (Kind of Blue, 1959)
genealogy.relationship: Modal jazz piano descends from bebop through a specific historical path: Davis's rejection of bebop chord changes in favor of modal scales (Kind of Blue), Tyner's quartal voicing innovation with Coltrane, Hancock's fusion of modal and funk harmony, and the academic codification of these innovations into teachable curriculum. Seales inherits this entire lineage. The piano theory branch extends from seven nodes (degrees 5-6 through 12) to eight nodes at degree 13, surpassing saxophone/reed as the richest branch. The thirteenth chromaticism deployment: modal chromaticism -- chromatic alterations within and between modal frameworks (raised fourth, natural sixth over minor, Lydian Dominant) as structural modal choices, not as passing tones or approach notes.

theory_nodes.concept: Academic jazz pedagogy (the university scholar-performer as integration point; DMA-level curriculum design; formal knowledge transmission vs. experiential transmission)
examples: Marc Seales -- UW School of Music, director of jazz studies; DMA; curriculum design, ensemble direction, graduate advising
Floyd Standifer (degree 4) -- UW faculty, experiential teaching model; the predecessor on the pedagogical arc
Julian Priester (degree 7) -- Cornish College of the Arts; working-musician teaching credential; institutional but not doctoral
Hadley Caliman (degree 9) -- Cornish College of the Arts; working-musician teaching from performance experience
Berklee College of Music -- the national model for institutional jazz education; comparison point for UW program
theory_nodes.description: Academic jazz pedagogy as practiced by the university scholar-performer represents the most formalized mode of jazz knowledge transmission in the engine. The DMA-level educator designs curriculum, directs ensembles, supervises graduate research, and articulates the theoretical frameworks that explain the music -- connecting performing knowledge (how modal voicings feel under the hands at tempo) with scholarly knowledge (why Dorian mode produces a particular emotional quality, and the historical context of its deployment). This is the seventh pedagogical mode in the engine: university scholar-performer. It completes the Standifer-to-Seales arc -- from experiential teaching rooted in bandstand apprenticeship (Standifer, degree 4) to doctoral-level academic program direction (Seales, degree 13). The institutional model is distinguished from all previous models by its capacity for institutional reproduction: the program generates its own successors through degree granting, creating a self-perpetuating pedagogical structure that does not depend on any single individual.

genealogy.relationship: The pedagogical models form a spectrum from person-dependent to institution-dependent knowledge transmission. Brazil's salon (degree 11) is entirely person-dependent -- when Brazil dies, the salon dies, and knowledge survives only in students present. Standifer's experiential teaching (degree 4) is partially institution-dependent (UW appointment) but relies on the teacher's personal presence and performance. Seales's university scholar-performer model (degree 13) is the most institution-dependent: the UW jazz program has curriculum, degree requirements, faculty positions, and administrative structure that can persist beyond any individual teacher. This node introduces the institution-dependency spectrum as a new analytical axis in the engine.

theory_nodes.concept: Piano lineage -- three eras (stride/cocktail -> hard bop/orchestral -> modal/contemporary; autodidact -> undergraduate -> doctoral; the full arc of jazz piano evolution and education in Seattle)
examples: Overton Berry (degree 5) -- stride/cocktail, self-taught, The Persistence, energy 2, 1940s-2020s
Patti Bown (degree 6) -- hard bop/orchestral, Seattle University, The Erasure, energy 3, 1950s-1970s
Marc Seales (degree 13) -- modal/contemporary, UW DMA, The Institutional Anchor, energy 2, 1990s-present
McCoy Tyner -- the quartal voicing innovation that Seales inherits (national context)
Bud Powell -- the bebop piano vocabulary that Bown inherited and that Seales's modal vocabulary evolved beyond (national context)
theory_nodes.description: Three Seattle jazz pianists across three eras document the full evolution of jazz piano style and jazz education. Berry (degree 5): stride and cocktail tradition, self-taught, the oldest vocabulary rooted in 1920s-1940s swing language. Bown (degree 6): hard bop and orchestral approach, Seattle University undergraduate training, the 1950s-1960s vocabulary of harmonic density. Seales (degree 13): modal and contemporary language, UW doctoral training, the post-Coltrane vocabulary of open voicings and theoretical awareness. The educational progression maps the entire spectrum of American jazz education: autodidact (Berry) -> undergraduate (Bown) -> doctorate (Seales). The piano theory branch extends to eight nodes at degree 13, becoming the richest branch in the engine.

ancestors: Bebop piano vocabulary (Bud Powell, referenced at degree 12)
Modal piano innovation (Tyner, Hancock, Corea, Jarrett -- national lineage)
genealogy.relationship: The three pianists form a direct lineage within the engine: Berry's stride vocabulary was the standard Seattle jazz piano sound for decades; Bown's hard bop approach represented the modernization of that vocabulary in the 1950s-1960s; Seales's modal/contemporary approach represents the current state of the art. The educational escalation (self-taught -> undergraduate -> doctoral) parallels the stylistic evolution and maps the broader professionalization of jazz education in America. Eight piano theory nodes now -- surpassing saxophone/reed (seven) as the richest branch.

theory_nodes.concept: Blue note -- modal deployment (thirteenth deployment; blue note as modal color choice within Dorian, Mixolydian, and minor-mode frameworks)
examples: Marc Seales -- modal blue note phrasing in contemporary jazz piano; Dorian natural sixth as modal color, b7 as Mixolydian identity
McCoy Tyner -- quartal voicings incorporating blue-note positions as structural modal elements
Herbie Hancock -- 'Maiden Voyage': blue notes as modal atmosphere rather than blues inflection
Miles Davis -- 'So What': Dorian mode with b3 and b7 as defining modal pitches, not as chromatic blue-note bends
theory_nodes.description: Thirteenth deployment of the blue note thread. In modal jazz, the blue note undergoes a conceptual transformation: the b3, b7, and natural-6-over-minor that were blues inflections in earlier styles become defining pitches of modal frameworks. In Dorian mode (the most common jazz minor mode), the b3 and b7 are not bent toward or approached chromatically -- they ARE the mode. The blue note is no longer a departure from the harmonic framework; it IS the harmonic framework. This is the most fully integrated blue-note deployment in the engine: where bebop (degree 12) systematized the blue note through chromatic approach patterns, modal jazz absorbs the blue note into the scale itself, making it a structural element rather than an inflection. The modal blue note is the endpoint of a thirteen-degree evolution from expressive gesture (vocal bend, trombone slide) to structural identity (the mode is built from blue notes).

ancestors: Blue note -- bebop chromatic approach (degree 12, Lanphere)
Blue note -- all twelve prior deployments (degrees 0-12)
genealogy.relationship: Thirteen deployments across fourteen degrees. The modal blue note is the most fully integrated mechanism: the blue note IS the mode, not a departure from it. Dorian's b3 and b7 are not approached chromatically (bebop) or bent expressively (blues vocal) -- they define the scale. This deployment reverses the blue note's historical status: from 'wrong' note bent toward correctness, to 'right' note that defines the harmonic identity. Thirteen deployments: bass-line (0), guitar (1), vocal (3), trumpet (4), piano crush (5), altered voicings (6), trombone slide (7), bass fundamental (8), sax voice (9), alto cool bend (10), flute bent note (11), bebop chromatic approach (12), modal integration (13).

seattle_connection: Born in Seattle -- native-born musician whose entire career and educational path (through DMA) is rooted in the Seattle metropolitan area. University of Washington School of Music faculty, director of jazz studies. The institutional anchor of Seattle jazz education. Collaborations with Julian Priester (degree 7) and other Seattle musicians position Seales at the center of the city's contemporary jazz network. The Institutional Anchor pattern: native-born, educated locally through terminal degree, permanent institutional appointment, active performer. Energy-2 profile -- academic warmth, community-embedded through both university and performance worlds. Eighth energy-2 artist.

theory_genealogy: Modal jazz piano vocabulary (Dorian, Mixolydian, Lydian, quartal voicings, extended chords, Lydian Dominant) -- eighth node on the piano theory branch, now the richest in the engine; inherits from bebop (degree 12) through the Davis/Coltrane/Tyner modal revolution

Academic jazz pedagogy (university scholar-performer, DMA-level curriculum design, institutional reproduction) -- seventh pedagogical mode; completes the Standifer-to-Seales arc; introduces institution-dependency spectrum
Piano lineage (Berry/stride -> Bown/hard bop -> Seales/modal; autodidact -> undergraduate -> doctoral) -- the full arc of Seattle jazz piano evolution and education
Blue note -- thirteenth deployment; modal integration where blue notes become defining pitches of the mode rather than departures from the harmonic framework; the most fully integrated deployment

key_albums.title: Marc Seales -- Naim Records releases
key_albums.label: Naim
key_albums.significance: Naim Records (UK) is an audiophile label whose jazz catalog emphasizes recording quality. The label's audio engineering ethos treats the recording as a high-fidelity document of the performance. Seales's Naim recordings document his contemporary modal piano vocabulary with exceptional audio fidelity. Second international label in the engine after Fresh Sound (degree 12). First UK-based label.

key_albums.title: Collaborative recordings with Seattle musicians
key_albums.label: Various
key_albums.significance: Seales's collaborative recordings with Julian Priester (degree 7) and other Seattle musicians document the contemporary Seattle jazz network. These recordings capture the intersection of academic and performance communities that defines Seales's unique position.

degree_neighbors.name: Overton Berry
degree_neighbors.relationship: Piano lineage (first chair). Berry's stride/cocktail vocabulary is the oldest piano tradition in the engine. Self-taught. Seales is the third pianist, the most contemporary and most formally educated. From autodidact to doctorate across three degrees.

degree_neighbors.name: Patti Bown
degree_neighbors.relationship: Piano lineage (second chair). Bown's hard bop/orchestral approach is the middle generation. Seattle University training. Educational escalation: Berry (self-taught) -> Bown (undergraduate) -> Seales (doctoral).

degree_neighbors.name: Floyd Standifer
degree_neighbors.relationship: Pedagogical arc. Standifer was UW faculty teaching through experience and community presence. Seales is UW faculty teaching through academic scholarship and institutional program design. The arc from experiential teaching to doctoral pedagogy at the same institution.

degree_neighbors.name: Julian Priester
degree_neighbors.relationship: Collaborator and fellow educator. Priester at Cornish, Seales at UW -- two institutional jazz programs in Seattle, each anchored by a working musician. Priester arrived from outside (Chicago); Seales is native-born.

degree_neighbors.name: Don Lanphere
degree_neighbors.relationship: Bebop genealogy. Lanphere's bebop vocabulary (1940s) is the ancestor of the modal vocabulary Seales deploys. Bebop -> hard bop/cool -> modal -> contemporary post-bop. Seales inherits from the tradition Lanphere helped create.

garfield_thread.degree_0: Quincy Jones -- attended late 1940s, class of 1950. Arranger/producer. Left Seattle.
garfield_thread.degree_3: Ernestine Anderson -- attended late 1940s, same era as Jones. Vocalist. Left and returned.
garfield_thread.degree_4: Floyd Standifer -- Central District post-WWII, educational path not confirmed as Garfield. Multi-instrumentalist and educator. Never left.
garfield_thread.degree_5: Overton Berry -- Central District native, self-taught. No institutional music education. Autodidact path.
garfield_thread.degree_6: Patti Bown -- Central District native, Seattle University (not Garfield). Formal training via Jesuit university.
garfield_thread.degree_7: Julian Priester -- NOT a Garfield product. DuSable High School (Chicago) under Captain Dyett. Cornish College of the Arts faculty.
garfield_thread.degree_8: Gary Peacock -- NOT a Garfield product. Burley, Idaho. Westlake College (LA). U.S. Army (Germany).
garfield_thread.degree_9: Hadley Caliman -- NOT a Garfield product. Idabel, Oklahoma. Los Angeles jazz scene. Cornish College of the Arts faculty.
garfield_thread.degree_10: Bud Shank -- NOT a Garfield product. Dayton, Ohio. University of North Carolina. Stan Kenton Orchestra. Los Angeles / Pacific Jazz.
garfield_thread.degree_11: Joe Brazil -- NOT a Garfield product. Detroit, Michigan. Detroit jazz scene. Settled in Central District as salon-model educator.
garfield_thread.degree_12: Don Lanphere -- NOT a Garfield product. Wenatchee, Washington. Northwestern University. NYC 52nd Street bebop scene.
garfield_thread.degree_13: Marc Seales -- NOT confirmed as Garfield product. Seattle native. University of Washington (DMA). Seventh consecutive non-Garfield degree. First UW-complete pipeline.

cultural_patterns.institution_type: the institutional anchor -- native-born, DMA from UW, UW School of Music faculty and director of jazz studies, active performer, the most institution-dependent pedagogical position in the engine
